figures.caption: Table 2: Caption accuracy (B4− CS) and caption consistency across multiple views of the same object (Mean CS − IQR) on HM3D [21] manually annotated test set.
Table 6: Parameters used during trajectory collection.

Fig. 1: Memory-driven multi-view exploration progressively resolves ambiguous object captions into a consistent object-level description. (a) the agent predicts a caption for the observed object; (b) a different caption is predicted from a different viewpoint; (c) a consistent caption is predicted based on the episodic object memory; (d) the predicted caption for the object remains consistent.
Fig. 5: Mean and standard deviation of memory token count (left) and number of objects (right) per step, averaged over all HM3D test episodes.
Fig. 4: Comparison of object captions predicted by a VLM baseline (Qwen3-VL) and our method along successive viewpoints of an exploration. Mistakes highlighted in red.

Fig. 6: Examples of episodic trajectories on scenes from the HM3D test set. (a),(b),(c): the agent successfully revisits one or more objects from different viewpoints, while avoiding obstacles. (d): partial navigation failure where the agent hits a wall.

Fig. 3: Mean and standard deviation of inference time (left) and memory occupancy (right) averaged over HM3D [21] test episodes for EPOS-VLM and the dense point cloud baseline [8]. KEY: Point cloud, Ours
Fig. 7: Additional qualitative examples for the vanilla and fine-tuned model of captions predicted over three views of the same object. Errors are highlighted in red.

Fig. 2: Overview of the proposed EPOS-VLM model. Structured episodic memory, RGB observations (with detected object bounding boxes and IDs overlayed), and top-down maps are jointly encoded and processed by a Vision–Language transformer, which autoregressively outputs object association, object-level captions, and actions.
